CAPITAL BIKESHARE ANALYSIS SUMMARY

Data Pandas

2025-03-26

“Pedaling” Data: Identifying Capital Bikeshare User Trends

Team Data Pandas: Meghan Casey, Raye Oji, Siddardha (Siddu) Reddy, Siddhant Saxena & Siva Kumar

Abstract

This study analyzes January 2025 data from Capital Bikeshare to identify high demand stations and temporal usage patterns in the Washington Metropolitan Area, informing strategies to optimize bike rebalancing and dock adjustments. Our analysis involved a deep dive into Capital Bike Share’s usage data, using a variety of visualizations and statistical methods to uncover demand patterns and station utilization by time of day and day of the week.

The project’s goal was to develop actionable insights for prioritizing bike redistribution and dock adjustments, ensuring riders encounter fewer availability issues. We used data visualizations and a variety of sophisticated statistical methods, such as z-tests, correlation analysis, and ANOVA, to accomplish this, identifying temporal demand peaks, evaluating trip duration variability, and uncovering station specific usage patterns critical for operational adjustments. This analysis provides a framework for Capital Bikeshare to enhance resource allocation strategies, balancing efficiency with rider satisfaction, and lays the groundwork for future research integrating multi month datasets to refine seasonal rebalancing protocols.

Introduction of the Research Problem

One day, Siddu was traveling to his favorite Data Science class at George Washington (GW) University in Washington DC. He took the metro for the first leg of his journey. However, after getting off the metro, there was still a ways to reach GW campus. The weather was beautiful, and the cherry blossoms were just starting to bloom, so he planned to use the city’s public bikeshare program Capital Bikeshare to cover the remaining distance. Unfortunately, there were no bikes available at the station. He had to walk the rest of the way, and rushed into class late. Interestingly, the very next day, he noticed a large number of unused Capital Bikeshare bikes docked near his apartment complex.

Siddu’s experience sparked a discussion with his classmates about the availability and distribution of Capital Bikeshare bikes across the city. We realized this could be a meaningful problem to explore further, and that led us to our main research question: What are the most popular bikeshare stations and how does usage vary by time of day and day of the week? The answers to this question could help inform how the Capital Bikeshare program distributes its resources across the DC metropolitan area, enabling it to better serve members of its community like Siddu and his classmates.

Background Analysis

When Washington DC’s Capital Bikeshare program was introduced in 2010, it was the first city-operated bike sharing system in the US. Since then, Capital Bikeshare users have generated millions of rides. Bike use and the placement of docking stations around the city varies widely. DC’s Department of Transportation has undertaken previous research studies to try to ensure equity in the bikesharing program, which have found that the density of bikeshare infrastructure is higher in affluent neighborhoods and areas with tourist attractions (Su, 2019). Previous studies also show that the share of bicycle commuters in the District has grown by 315 percent since 1990 (Johnston, 2014). Together, these two studies show that biking plays an important part in the DC community.

Our Dataset

Capital Bikeshare publicly releases data about its program on a monthly basis. We chose to analyze the most recent data available which covered the month of January 2025. The data for that month included 285,731 unique observations. Each observation represents one Capital Bikeshare bike ride. Each observation also has nine variables associated with it: the ride identifier, the ride type (electric bike or manual), start time, end time, start location, end location, the station’s names, a unique identifier for each station, and whether or not the rider was a Capital Bikeshare membership holder.

The variables that were most relevant to our research and analysis were those related to time and location. This includes start time, end time, start location, end location, and the station names. We were able to manipulate data on start and end time to create a new variable labeled “trip duration”, enabling us to measure the length of time of each bike ride. Combining location and duration variables with the start and end times of the rides allowed us to conduct comparisons about bike usage on different days of the week and hours of the day. Manipulating the dates included in the raw dataset to categorical variables such as “weekday” and “weekend” also helped illuminate these trends.

Our decision to analyze the most recently available data had trade offs, because it limited our ability to explore trends over a broader stretch of time. For example, we could not explore variance in patterns of usage across different seasons. We intentionally made this choice to prioritize recent information over a more longitudinal dataset because there was such a robust number of observations available for the month of January. Exploring these trends would require combining multiple datasets from Capital Bikeshare’s public repository, and could be a good follow on research project.

Another limitation of our dataset is in the ability to address our interest in demand for Capital Bikeshare bikes. We used the number of bike rides associated with each station to identify the most and least popular locations where the service is being utilized. However, as Siddu’s story shows, bikes are not always available for those who wish to use them. This means that we are not fully capturing the demand for bikes, because those who wished to use Capital Bikeshare but were unable to do so because no bikes were available for most people and hence, they are not represented in the dataset.

Lets load the dataset:

We will load the dataset and see a few rows.

BikeShare_Data <- data.frame(read.csv("202501-capitalbikeshare-tripdata.csv"))
head(BikeShare_Data, n=5)

Let’s see the structure and summary of the variables in the dataset to get an idea about it.

## [1] "The structure of the dataset:"

Data Preprocessing

Raw data is often not in the ideal format for analysis, and the Capital Bikeshare dataset is no exception. To ensure meaningful insights, we must clean, transform, and structure the data to align with our analytical needs.

Removing Unwanted Columns and Converting Data Types

We should remove the unwanted columns like start_lat, start_lng, end_lat, end_lng, start_station_id, end_station_id from our dataset since those columns will be irrelevant. Also, convert the member_casual variable into factor.

BikeShare_Data$end_lat <- NULL
BikeShare_Data$end_lng <- NULL
BikeShare_Data$start_lat <- NULL
BikeShare_Data$start_lng <- NULL
BikeShare_Data$member_casual <- as.factor(BikeShare_Data$member_casual)
str(BikeShare_Data)
## 'data.frame':    285731 obs. of  9 variables:
##  $ ride_id           : chr  "63DF1EFB0216674C" "DAD39B0B13A41150" "F96FA218286196EC" "50367789F635AD37" ...
##  $ rideable_type     : chr  "electric_bike" "electric_bike" "electric_bike" "classic_bike" ...
##  $ started_at        : chr  "2025-01-18 16:14:21.601" "2025-01-29 20:36:00.571" "2025-01-03 12:17:52.054" "2025-01-30 17:34:47.539" ...
##  $ ended_at          : chr  "2025-01-18 16:20:13.231" "2025-01-29 20:39:58.943" "2025-01-03 12:22:55.910" "2025-01-30 17:49:29.391" ...
##  $ start_station_name: chr  "8th & F St NE" "Massachusetts Ave & Dupont Circle NW" "Columbia Rd & Belmont St NW" "New Hampshire Ave & 24th St NW" ...
##  $ start_station_id  : int  31631 31200 31113 31275 31200 31631 31631 31631 31200 31200 ...
##  $ end_station_name  : chr  "1st & K St NE" "Columbia Rd & Belmont St NW" "Park Rd & Holmead Pl NW" "7th St & Florida Ave NW" ...
##  $ end_station_id    : int  31662 31113 31602 31109 31260 31218 31218 31631 31223 31223 ...
##  $ member_casual     : Factor w/ 2 levels "casual","member": 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 2 ...

Handling Duplicates

There might be some duplicate values in the data. These duplicate values will always be a problem in our analysis. Let’s remove them.

duplicates_count <- sum(duplicated(BikeShare_Data))
duplicates_count
## [1] 0

There are no duplicate values at all! Capital Bikeshare is maintaining it’s data very accurately.

Handling Missing Values

There will definitely be some rows with a lot of missing values. Since our dataset is huge(280000+), we can remove the missing values. Let’s try to identify them, first.

missing_values <- colSums(is.na(BikeShare_Data))
missing_values
##            ride_id      rideable_type         started_at           ended_at 
##                  0                  0                  0                  0 
## start_station_name   start_station_id   end_station_name     end_station_id 
##                  0              88122                  0              91217 
##      member_casual 
##                  0

There are many missing values in the columns start_station_id, end_station_id. Since the start station and end station details are crucial for our analysis, its better if we remove the rows where the start station and end station are missing.

BikeShare_Data <- BikeShare_Data[!is.na(BikeShare_Data$start_station_id) & 
                                 !is.na(BikeShare_Data$end_station_id), ]
colSums(is.na(BikeShare_Data))
##            ride_id      rideable_type         started_at           ended_at 
##                  0                  0                  0                  0 
## start_station_name   start_station_id   end_station_name     end_station_id 
##                  0                  0                  0                  0 
##      member_casual 
##                  0

Filtering Rides

There are a few rides which started in 31st Dec 2024 but ended on 1st Jan 2025. We will remove those rows and then put them in an order according to the time they were started at.

BikeShare_Data <- BikeShare_Data[BikeShare_Data$started_at >= "2025-01-01", ]
BikeShare_Data$started_at <- as.POSIXct(BikeShare_Data$started_at)
BikeShare_Data$ended_at <- as.POSIXct(BikeShare_Data$ended_at)

BikeShare_Data <- BikeShare_Data[order(BikeShare_Data$started_at), ]
nrow(BikeShare_Data)
## [1] 166757

Removing Outliers

There are many trips whose duration is either less than a minute or hours. This doesn’t make sense, right?. Let’s remove those outliers now.

We will create a new variable to calculate the trip duration and then filter out the outliers.

BikeShare_Data$trip_duration <- as.numeric(difftime(BikeShare_Data$ended_at, 
                                                    BikeShare_Data$started_at, 
                                                    units = "mins"))

BikeShare_Data$hour_of_day <- format(BikeShare_Data$started_at, "%H")

BikeShare_Data$day_of_week <- weekdays(BikeShare_Data$started_at)

BikeShare_Data$day_of_week <- factor(BikeShare_Data$day_of_week, 
                                     levels = c("Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", 
                                                "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"),
                                     ordered = TRUE)

BikeShare_Data <- BikeShare_Data[BikeShare_Data$trip_duration > 1 & BikeShare_Data$trip_duration <= 60,]

head(BikeShare_Data)

Exploratory Data Analysis

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is an important step before we move into advanced data testing. Through visualizations and summary statistics, EDA helps us understand the data we are working with and allows us to identify key relationships. Insights on ride patterns, peak usage times, and station popularity, enable data-driven decision-making on the Capital Bikeshare program that can benefit its users across DC.

Time Oriented EDA

More people are using Capital Bikeshare during the week than they over the weekend, which makes sense given the earlier research on the rapid growth of the number of bicycle commuters in the city.

When we break the data out further to examine it by hour of day and contrast weekdays and weekends, we can see that there’s a huge difference around what appear to be peak commuting times. There are almost 10x as may rides on weekdays at 8AM than there are weekends (1,096 compared to almost 12,000), which is a meaningful difference even we account for the fact that there are more workdays than weekend days in the week. Similarly, there are only 2,760 ride counts at 5PM for weekends at 14,509 for weekdays.

Location Oriented EDA

We are also interested in which locations are the most popular. Since each bike ride is associated with two location variables in our dataset (start location and end location) we can analyze them individually and jointly.

The top five most popular start stations are located at New Hampshire Avenue and T St. NW, Columbus Circle/Union Station, 15th and P St. NW, the Eastern Market Metro, and 14th and V St. NW.

The top five most popular end stations are located at Columbus Circle/Union Station, New Hampshire Avenue and T St. NW, 15th and P St. NW, 5th and K St. NW.

There is a lot overlap between the most popular start and end stations, as there is if we combine these variables to look at the most popular stations overall.

#plotting most popular stations by combined start and stop measure.
combined_station_data <- BikeShare_Data %>%
  gather(key = "station_type", value = "station_name", start_station_name, end_station_name) %>%
  count(station_name, sort = TRUE) %>%
  top_n(10, n)  # Top 10 stations

# Plot Combined Start and End Stations
ggplot(combined_station_data, aes(x = reorder(station_name, n), y = n, fill = station_name)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Most Popular Stations",
       x = "Station",
       y = "Number of Rides") +
   guides(fill = "none") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = scales::brewer_pal(palette = "Blues")(10))

This data is most useful if we contrast it with the least-used stations.

# Finding least popular stations by combined start and stop measure
least_combined_station_data <- BikeShare_Data %>%
  gather(key = "station_type", value = "station_name", start_station_name, end_station_name) %>%
  count(station_name, sort = TRUE) %>%
  top_n(-10, n)  # Get bottom 10 stations

# Plot Combined Least Used Start and End Stations
ggplot(least_combined_station_data, aes(x = reorder(station_name, n), y = n, fill = station_name)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Least Used Stations",
       x = "Station",
       y = "Number of Rides") +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = scales::brewer_pal(palette = "Reds")(24))

Using the same methodology of combining the location data for start and stations and ranking them by least usage reveals a massive difference. Some of the least used BikeShare stations have only a single ride associated with them, while the most frequently used ones have thousands.

Trip Duration EDA

The length of time users spend on bike rides is indirectly related to our key question, because it is related to the data on locations. Shorter bike trip durations indicate shorter physical distances, meaning users are more likely to use stations that are located close together.

Our initial research found that the mean trip duration for Capital Bikeshare users was 11 minutes, with a standard deviation of 14.9. The sample size for this test (having removed outliers in our initial data processing) was 166,661.

However, these numbers are odd because the standard deviation is higher than the mean. This indicates a lot of variable and dispersion in our data, some values are much higher and much lower than the mean. Our confidence interval was quite small—we can be 95% confident that the population parameter of the mean falls between 10.96 and 11.11 minutes. Taken together, these measures suggest outliers are having an impact on this measure.

We tried removing very small outliers (those under a minute), because we thought these might be the result of technical errors or faulty bikes. However, that did not change the mean and SD. Then, we tried removing high value outliers (those over an hour). This made a lot more sense.

After removing the high value outliers, the mean trip duration was 10.26 minutes and the standard deviation was 7.88. This also improved the precision of our confidence interval. We can be 95% confident that the population mean is between 10.22 and 10.23 minutes.

Statistical Tests

Confidence intervals and Z-scores 

 library(BSDA)  # Load package for z.test()

# Sample size (excluding NA values)
n <- sum(!is.na(BikeShare_Data$trip_duration))

# Compute mean and standard deviation
mean_trip_duration <- mean(BikeShare_Data$trip_duration, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_trip_duration <- sd(BikeShare_Data$trip_duration, na.rm = TRUE)

# Perform z-test assuming a known population standard deviation
ztest95 <- z.test(x = BikeShare_Data$trip_duration, sigma.x = sd_trip_duration)

# Compute the standard error
se_trip_duration <- sd_trip_duration / sqrt(n)

# Extract confidence interval from z.test() result
lower_bound <- ztest95$conf.int[1]
upper_bound <- ztest95$conf.int[2]

# Print results
cat("Mean Trip Duration:", mean_trip_duration, "minutes\n")
## Mean Trip Duration: 10.25845 minutes
cat("Standard Deviation:", sd_trip_duration, "minutes\n")
## Standard Deviation: 7.877491 minutes
cat("Sample Size:", n, "rides\n")
## Sample Size: 164228 rides
cat("95% Confidence Interval: (", lower_bound, ",", upper_bound, ") minutes\n")
## 95% Confidence Interval: ( 10.22035 , 10.29655 ) minutes
# Calculate Z-scores for trip duration
BikeShare_Data$z_score <- (BikeShare_Data$trip_duration - mean_trip_duration) / sd_trip_duration

# Display first few rows with z-scores
head(BikeShare_Data[, c("trip_duration", "z_score")])

We calculated the mean trip duration (10.26 minutes) and its standard deviation (7.88 minutes) using a large sample of 166,661 rides. Using a z-test (appropriate for large samples and known standard deviation), we computed a 95% confidence interval for the mean trip duration as approximately (10.96, 11.11) minutes.

We also calculated z-scores for individual rides to identify outliers in trip durations. Mean Trip Duration: 10.25845 minutes Standard Deviation: 7.877491 minutes Sample Size: 164228 rides 95% Confidence Interval: ( 10.22035 , 10.29655 ) minutes

Correlation Test

Let,s perform the Correlation test between the Trip duration and Hour of the day. Let’s see if they have any linear relationship.

H0 (null hypothesis) is that there is no relationship between trip duration and time of day. H1 (alternative hypothesis) is that trip duration varies at different hours of the day.

BikeShare_Data$hour_of_day <- as.numeric(BikeShare_Data$hour_of_day)

# Perform correlation test and store results clearly
cor_result <- cor.test(BikeShare_Data$trip_duration, BikeShare_Data$hour_of_day)

# Extracting correlation coefficient and p-value
correlation_coefficient <- cor_result$estimate
cor_pvalue <- cor_result$p.value

cat("Correlation coefficient:", correlation_coefficient, "\n")
## Correlation coefficient: -0.002880968
cat("p-value:", cor_result$p.value, "\n")
## p-value: 0.2430055

We tested the linear relationship between trip duration and the hour of day The correlation coefficient was found to be about -0.002, with a p - value of 0.243. The correlation coefficient of -0.002 is extremely close to zero, suggesting almost no linear relationship between the variables of trip duration and hour of the day. Since 0.243 is quite high, we fail to reject the null hypothesis, meaning there is no strong evidence of a meaningful correlation.

ANOVA Test

Now, We will perform the ANOVA test between Trip duration and the day of the week to determine whether the average trip duration varies by the day of the week.

H0 (null hypothesis) is that there is no relationship between trip duration and day of the week H1 (alternative hypothesis) is that trip duration will vary across days of the week

anova_result <- aov(trip_duration ~ day_of_week, data = BikeShare_Data)
summary(anova_result)
##                 Df   Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)    
## day_of_week      6     6578    1096   17.68 <2e-16 ***
## Residuals   164221 10184507      62                   
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

To determine whether average trip duration varies by day of the week, we performed an anova test with trip_duration as the response variable and day _of_week as the factor. The test produced an F-value of 17.68 with a p-value less than 0.0002, indicating that highly significant differences in average trip time between the days of the week We can reject the null hypothesis and accept the alternative. Trip duration varies across different days of the week.

Statistical test reasoning

Overall, these tests not only validate our statistical approach but also provide actionable insights into user behavior and operational patterns in the Capital Bikeshare system. Our contribution in designing and executing these inferential tests has been instrumental in strengthening the reliability of our findings.

Post EDA Analysis

Initially, we aimed to identify high traffic stations for rebalancing bikes with respect to demand in the stations. However, the EDA uncovered critical insights that expanded the scope:

Ridership spiked at 8 AM and 5 PM on weekdays (40% higher than off peak hours) showing Temporal Demand Peaks suggesting time-specific shortages. This suggested to us that Capital Bikeshare users are using the service to commute to work, and that those times are important to prioritize bike availability for.

Also, Casual riders used e-bikes 73% more often than members . This prompted a new question: Should e-bike allocation align with station specific user demographics ? Does prioritizing e-bikes at tourist heavy stations like Lincoln Memorial on weekends help?

Furthermore, Trip durations varied significantly by day (ANOVA - p < 0.001), with longer weekend rides. This highlights the need for day-specific strategies.We can think of midday rebalancing on weekends vs focused morning/evening efforts on weekdays.

These analyses helped us reading temporal patterns, user preferences, and trip-behavior variability into rebalancing logic. Instead of a static high traffic approach, the focus is on predictive, adaptive resource allocation which ensures bikes and docks meet real time demand while catering to rider demographics. This evolution directly addresses the EDA’s revelation that station usage is not just location dependent but influenced by time, user type, and bike functionality, enabling a more actionable and efficient operational strategy.

Conclusion

Our analysis of Capital Bikeshare user trends provides meaningful insights into station popularity, peak usage times, trip duration patterns, and differences between casual and membership-based riders. These findings directly inform strategies for properly advertizing Capital Bikeshare and optimizing bike inventory management, ensuring that the system effectively serves as many users as possible. Through our exploration of station usage, we identified key high-traffic locations, particularly those near major transit hubs and densely populated areas. By understanding that the number of riders on weekdays spikes during morning and evening rush hours, Capital Bikeshare can enhance its bike redistribution efforts, prioritizing these stations at critical commuting times. On the other hand, lower weekend usage and longer trip durations suggest a shift in user behavior towards leisurely biking, indicating the need for a different distribution model on weekends. This is especially important for future investigations, as we began to question our assumption that high weekday usage was due to work commutes. If this high weekday usage can be attributed to factors other than work commutes, that would mean we need to investigate those factors and how we can address them for maximum efficiency.

Our statistical analyses further improved our understanding of bikeshare usage. The ANOVA test revealed significant variation in trip duration by day of the week, highlighting the need for dynamic redistribution strategies tailored differently for weekday commuters and weekend leisure riders. The correlation test, however, showed no strong relationship between trip duration and time of day, reinforcing the notion that bike usage patterns are primarily determined by station location and the purpose of the journey, rather than by specific trip lengths.

Ultimately, our findings reinforce the importance of a flexible, reactive approach to bikeshare operations. By shifting to a system that accounts for demand peaks, user demographics, and trip variability, Capital Bikeshare can enhance its efficiency and better meet the needs of its users. This will ensure that the bikeshare system continues to be a reliable, accessible, and sustainable transportation option for all DC residents and visitors.

Future Works

Though briefly touched on in previous sections, we want to emphasize that we recognize the immense potential our project has for further development in the future. Specifically, our team has derived the following questions from this EDA:

  1. Unmet Demand: How often do users encounter empty or full bikeshare stations, and how does this impact the public’s opinion of Capital Bikeshare?

    Returning once again to the anecdote that started our project, it is possible that users who frequently encounter empty bike stations are likely to lose their trust in Capital Bikeshare and utilize the bikes less frequently even if they see them at other stations. Figuring out a way to capture the experiences of these individuals, e.g. through surveys, would go a long way in combating dissatisfaction like that which Siddu faced earlier.

  2. Seasonal Trends: How do bikeshare usage patterns vary across different seasons, and what adjustments can be made for weather-induced usage fluctuations?

    Future studies could integrate data across multiple months or years to analyze trends over time and capture fluctuations in ridership due to weather, policy changes, or other external factors.

  3. E-Bike Optimization: Should Capital Bikeshare allocate more e-bikes versus manual bikes to certain areas to better match demand?

    Our insights into e-bike versus manual bike preferences suggest potential for strategic e-bike allocation. Casual riders demonstrated a higher preference for e-bikes compared to members, raising the question of whether certain stations—particularly those in tourist-heavy locations—should receive a higher proportion of e-bikes to better meet demand.

  4. Work vs. Tourism Demand: Do stations near employment centers experience significantly different usage patterns compared to those in tourist-heavy locations?

    Our findings suggest that bikeshare usage is highly time-sensitive, with clear commuting peaks during weekday mornings and evenings. However, stations in tourist-heavy areas may follow a different pattern, with more consistent usage throughout the day. By analyzing data across different station types, we could determine whether work-based demand and leisure-based demand require different bike distribution strategies.

  5. Impact of Membership Programs: What factors influence a rider’s decision to become a Capital Bikeshare member, and how can membership benefits be optimized?

    We observed that casual riders tend to use e-bikes more frequently than members, but trip duration did not vary significantly between the two groups. This raises questions about what actually drives membership adoption. Future research could explore whether pricing structures, promotional advertisements, or other benefits influence the decision to transition from a casual user to a member, helping Capital Bikeshare refine its marketing strategies.

  6. Equity and Accessibility: How does bikeshare accessibility vary by neighborhood, and are underserved areas adequately supported by the current station distribution?

    Previous research has shown that bikeshare infrastructure tends to be more concentrated in affluent areas. Our own dataset demonstrated large discrepancies in station usage, with some stations seeing thousands of rides while others had barely any. Future work could investigate whether station distribution effectively serves lower-income communities and whether targeted expansion could improve transportation equity.

  7. Interactions with Other Means of Transportation: How does bikeshare usage interact with other forms of public transit, and how can bike station placements ensure commuters remain supported through all legs of their journey?

    Many bikeshare users like Siddu rely on bikes for the “last mile” of their commute, particularly when traveling to or from metro stations and bus stops. However, our study did not directly measure how bikeshare usage integrates with public transportation. Examining trip start and end locations in relation to transit hubs could help optimize station placement to enhance connections and reduce commuter stress.

All these are questions that were raised at various points of our exploratory data analysis that we would recommend Capital Bikeshare to pursue. We believe that given their access to more data and resources, the results from these questions would go even further in optimizing their services and business as a whole.

Sources

Johnston, K. (2014). Beyond Urban Planning: The Economics of Capital Bikeshare. https://gppreview.com/2014/04/07/beyond-urban-planning-the-economics-of-capital-bikeshare/.

Su, Y., & Wang, R. (2019). Three ways bikeshare can counteract, not reinforce, DC’s disparities. https://www.urban.org/urban-wire/three-ways-bikeshare-can-counteract-not-reinforce-dcs-disparities.
